explanation: The requirement specifies that before ventilation starts, the system must prompt the user to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds for SAV.3-SAV.9. The most direct atomic proposition related to this is 'enterAlarmThresholds' (for prompting/alarm thresholds entry), and 'ventilating' (for ventilation started). So, this requirement is best captured as: it is always the case that if ventilation starts ('ventilating'), then previously at some point before that, the user has been prompted to enter alarm thresholds ('enterAlarmThresholds'). This is typically expressed using a property that states: whenever 'ventilating' becomes true, then in the past (or at least at the same time as ventilation start), 'enterAlarmThresholds' was true. In classical LTL, this is usually reversed as: Whenever 'ventilating' is started, 'enterAlarmThresholds' should already have occurred or happen simultaneously. The template (G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3)) fits best, where bool_exp2 = 'ventilating' and bool_exp3 = 'enterAlarmThresholds'.

The requirement is that the system shall prompt the user before ventilation is started to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds for SAV.3- SAV.9. This means that, whenever ventilation is started (i.e., when 'ventilating' becomes true), it must be preceded, or immediately preceded, by the user being prompted to enter the alarm thresholds (modeled as 'enterAlarmThresholds'). To satisfy the causality (before) relation, we encode: 'If ventilation is started, then, at the same time or before, the entry of alarm thresholds has occurred.' Thus, we use the template (G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3)), where bool_exp1 is 'ventilating' and bool_exp3 is 'enterAlarmThresholds'. This means: always, if ventilation occurs, the alarm threshold entry also happened. This is the closest translation using the available templates and atomic propositions; it captures the intent that alarm thresholds must be entered before or at the same time as ventilation is started.

The given requirement mandates that before ventilation starts, the system should prompt the user to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds. The atomic proposition 'enterAlarmThresholds' directly represents the action of entering these thresholds, and 'ventilating' represents that ventilation has started. The temporal requirement is that whenever 'ventilating' becomes true (i.e., ventilation starts), the user must already have been prompted or completed 'enterAlarmThresholds' beforehand. This is best captured by the LTL template: G(ventilating -> enterAlarmThresholds), meaning that it is always true that if ventilation is started, the alarm thresholds were entered before (or, more strictly, that 'enterAlarmThresholds' happens at or before 'ventilating').

The requirement is for the user to be prompted to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds before ventilation is started. The proposition 'enterAlarmThresholds' indicates the user is entering alarm thresholds, and 'ventilating' indicates ventilation is occurring. The correct formalization is that, globally, whenever ventilation starts (i.e., at the rising edge of 'ventilating'), the system should already have prompted for and had the alarm thresholds entered by the user before ventilation commences. This is best expressed by ensuring that, once 'ventilating' becomes true, 'enterAlarmThresholds' must have occurred prior to (or at the same time as) that. In LTL, this is captured using template 120, which encodes: whenever there is a rising edge for 'ventilating' (i.e., it becomes true), then immediately in the next step, 'enterAlarmThresholds' must be true. This enforces the user's requirement that the system shall prompt (ensure alarm thresholds are entered) before ventilation starts.

The requirement specifies that before ventilation starts, the user must be prompted to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds for SAV.3 - SAV.9. In terms of available atomic propositions, 'ventilating' indicates that ventilation is taking place, and 'enterAlarmThresholds' indicates the entering of user-controlled alarm thresholds. To capture 'before ventilation is started', we express that whenever ventilation starts (i.e., 'ventilating' becomes true), it must be preceded by a prompt to enter alarm thresholds. The most appropriate template for this relationship is: 'G (( !A & X A ) -> X B ) & ( A -> B )' (template 20), which asserts that whenever 'ventilating' changes from false to true, 'enterAlarmThresholds' must hold in the next state (immediately after), and whenever 'ventilating' holds, 'enterAlarmThresholds' holds (so at ventilation start, the alarm thresholds have been entered).

The requirement states that the user must enter user-controlled alarm thresholds for SAV.3–SAV.9 before ventilation is started. In formal LTL, this means that whenever ventilation is started ('ventilating' becomes true), it must be the case that 'enterAlarmThresholds' has already been entered. This is best captured with the template 'G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3)', where bool_exp1 is 'ventilating' (the condition being checked), and bool_exp3 is 'enterAlarmThresholds' (the required condition). This globally enforces that if ventilation starts, user-entered alarm thresholds exist. Both variables are valid atomic propositions per the provided dictionary.

The input requires that before ventilation starts, the system must prompt the user to enter alarm thresholds for SAV.3- SAV.9. Among the atomic propositions, 'enterAlarmThresholds' signifies the action of entering user-controlled alarm thresholds and 'ventilating' signifies that ventilation is occurring. The LTL property should guarantee that any time ventilation starts, the user has just been prompted for alarm thresholds.

To capture the requirement that the system shall prompt for alarm thresholds before ventilation, we want: whenever ventilating becomes true (i.e., when ventilation starts), enterAlarmThresholds has just occurred. This is best represented using template 60, which ensures that when ventilating becomes true (could also be interpreted as every time ventilating becomes true, enterAlarmThresholds occurs in the same step or just before ventilation is triggered).

Thus, 'bool_exp1' = 'ventilating' (ventilation is/will be started) and 'bool_exp3' = 'enterAlarmThresholds' (user is prompted to enter thresholds).

This property therefore enforces that every time ventilation is begun, the user prompt for alarm threshold entry has just occurred.

The natural language states that before ventilation is started, the system must prompt the user to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds for SAV.3- SAV.9. Mapping this to atomic propositions, 'ventilating' represents that ventilation has started, and 'enterAlarmThresholds' represents the entry of user-controlled alarm thresholds. The correct LTL property should specify that it is always the case that if ventilation starts, the alarm thresholds must have already been entered beforehand. This is correctly expressed as globally: if 'ventilating' becomes true, then 'enterAlarmThresholds' must have happened before or at the same time. This is best captured by the LTL template with ID 128, which asserts that 'ventilating' must only become true when 'enterAlarmThresholds' has occurred. This captures the requested requirement that the system must ensure the user is prompted to enter alarm thresholds before ventilation starts.

The requirement is that before ventilation starts, the system must prompt the user to enter alarm thresholds for SAV.3-SAV.9. The atomic proposition 'enterAlarmThresholds' represents 'indicates enter user-controlled alarm thresholds', and 'ventilating' indicates that ventilation has started. To ensure that the user is prompted to enter thresholds before ventilation begins, for every instance where ventilation starts (i.e., 'ventilating' becomes true), it must be the case that 'enterAlarmThresholds' occurred immediately before (in the previous step), or at the same time. Template 41 expresses: G ((bool_exp1 & bool_exp2) -> (X bool_exp3)), which can capture 'if not ventilating before but ventilating now (i.e., ventilation is starting), in the next step (once started), the condition holds'. But since 'ventilating' being true at the present means 'ventilation has started', and we want to make sure that immediately prior, 'enterAlarmThresholds' was activated, it is correct to state 'whenever ventilation starts, the system must have just prompted for entering alarm thresholds'. Thus, we encode '((! ventilating) & (X ventilating))' meaning 'ventilation just started', and we require that in the current step, 'enterAlarmThresholds' holds, using template 60: ((G (((! bool_exp1) & (X bool_exp1)) -> (X bool_exp3))) & (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3)). Here, bool_exp1 = ventilating and bool_exp3 = enterAlarmThresholds. N_DURATION is not used. This property forces: whenever ventilation starts, the system had just prompted for alarm thresholds.

The natural language requirement specifies that the system must prompt the user to enter alarm thresholds before ventilation is started. Using the available atomic propositions, 'enterAlarmThresholds' captures the activity of entering the thresholds and 'ventilating' indicates that ventilation is ongoing. The requirement is a safety/sequence constraint: for every point in time, if ventilation starts, then the thresholds must have already been entered before or exactly at the same moment. The standard LTL encoding for such requirements is to ensure that 'ventilating' cannot be true unless 'enterAlarmThresholds' has occurred (either now or earlier). More directly, we require that at all times, 'ventilating' implies that 'enterAlarmThresholds' has already occurred. For this, the template G(bool_exp3 -> bool_exp1) is appropriate, mapping 'ventilating' to bool_exp3 and 'enterAlarmThresholds' to bool_exp1.

This LTL property guarantees that ventilation cannot occur unless the alarm thresholds have already (at any point in the past, including now) been entered by the user, effectively enforcing a prompt at or before the onset of ventilation, as required.
